body_txt: Not all heroes are fearless. Many, in fact, are noted for their nervousness, timidity, reluctance to go anywhere near danger — all the hallmarks of a coward. Yet when push comes to shove, they will always step up, doing whatever it takes to accomplish their objective, save their friends, and get everyone out alive — exactly unlike a Dirty Coward or an Accidental Hero . Unlike Took a Level in Badass , the Cowardly Lion doesn't put off their fearful ways after a single moment of glory. They remain nervous and/or insecure in their daily life despite the fact that they can kick major ass when put to it. But they never seek praise: they will probably tell everyone to Think Nothing of It , because they think a 'real' hero is never scared. The Cowardly Lion is likely to be a Combat Pragmatist who gets the job done indirectly, not risking a straight-on fight which they’ll probably lose. But however little they think of themselves, their True Companions value them for their courage. Usually, inverse of Miles Gloriosus . Unlike The So-Called Coward , the Cowardly Lion does not need to be viewed by other characters as cowardly; it is their own opinion that matters. (And then, the so-called coward may be perfectly confident in his own courage.) Unlike the Accidental Hero , the examples in this trope are characters who deliberately set out to save the day, fears be damned. Compare and Contrast Admitting to Fear , when a character not known for being scared will admit they're afraid. May overlap with Easy to Scare . Contrast the Fearless Fool , who is never afraid and therefore is never brave. This character type is prone to Heroic Self-Deprecation and a Face Your Fears plot. See also Lovable Coward . One possible way to describe a Weak, but Skilled Paper Tiger . Not unusual for this character to be a Stone Wall . Compare Heroic Neutral . One of the major subtropes of the Classical Anti-Hero . Although traditionally applied to heroic or noble characters (especially given who the Trope Namer is), there are also possible villainous or antagonistic examples, demonstrating that even those types of characters, no matter their degree of fearfulness, don't always have to prove themselves that they're Dirty Cowards .
